headline: Russia unleashes its biggest strike yet on Kyiv as Iran buries its assassinated Supreme Leader — while a weak US jobs report quietly flips the world's most important interest-rate question.
the_big_picture: Today the world feels caught between war and money. Two hot conflicts — Russia's war on Ukraine and the aftermath of the US-Israel war on Iran — both escalated in visible, human ways: a record missile barrage on Kyiv, and a week-long funeral spectacle in Tehran designed as a message of defiance. Underneath the headlines runs a quieter but huge shift: America's job engine is sputtering, which paradoxically cheered markets because it takes the threat of higher interest rates off the table and sent gold and blue-chip stocks up. For India — home — the mood is more anxious: the weakest June monsoon in over a century is drying out farms, foreign investors are pulling money out at a record pace, and the rupee sits near an all-time low, even as the stock market grinds higher. The throughline: geopolitics sets the risks, but it's the price of money — rates, currencies, oil — that quietly decides who wins and loses.

stories.title: Russia hits Kyiv with its biggest attack of the war
stories.background: Russia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine is now in its fifth year. After grinding front-line gains stalled, Moscow has increasingly turned to long-range aerial bombardment of cities, while Ukraine has hit back deep inside Russia — especially at oil refineries and border regions like Belgorod. Each side frames its strikes as retaliation for the other's.
stories.what_happened: Overnight into July 2, Russia fired roughly 74 missiles — including a record 28 ballistic missiles aimed at the capital — and around 496 drones at Kyiv, damaging about 130 buildings across 30 locations. Ukrainian authorities put the death toll at around 25–30 with more than 80–90 injured, making it the deadliest strike on Kyiv since May. A record 52,500 people, including nearly 4,500 children, sheltered in the city's metro stations.
stories.why_it_matters: This is one of the largest single assaults of the entire war and it deliberately targeted residential areas and civilian infrastructure, not just military sites. It signals that despite on-and-off diplomacy, Russia is escalating pressure on Ukrainian civilians — and that Ukraine's improving air defences and counterattacks have not stopped the bombardment.
stories.ripple_effects: Heavier attacks raise the stakes for Ukraine's Western backers, who must keep supplying air-defence interceptors that are expensive and finite. Poland scrambled jets and Finland restricted airspace as the strikes spread fear across NATO's eastern flank. Continued attacks on energy sites — on both sides — keep a floor under global oil and gas prices, which feeds into inflation everywhere, including India's fuel bill.
stories.why_now: Russia's Defence Ministry called it retaliation for recent Ukrainian drone strikes on Russian oil and energy infrastructure — part of a tit-for-tat cycle of deep strikes that has intensified this summer as front-line advances slow to a near-halt.
stories.watch_next: Whether Western governments respond with more air-defence pledges, and whether Ukraine retaliates against Russian energy sites again — the trigger for the next escalation.

key_terms.definition: A missile that flies very high and fast on an arcing path, making it much harder to shoot down than a slow drone — which is why a record number of them in one attack is significant.
Systems (radars and interceptor missiles) that detect and shoot down incoming missiles and drones; interceptors are costly and can run short in a sustained barrage.

stories.the_lesson: In modern warfare, the side that runs out of cheap defences first is often the one that loses the war of attrition — a $50,000 drone can force the use of a $2 million interceptor, so cost, not just courage, decides how long a country can hold on.
stories.market_link: Sustained strikes on Russian and Ukrainian energy infrastructure keep upward pressure on oil and European natural-gas prices, which lifts energy stocks and feeds inflation.

stories.title: Iran buries its assassinated Supreme Leader in a week-long show of defiance
stories.background: Ali Khamenei ruled Iran as Supreme Leader — its highest political and religious authority — for 36 years. He was assassinated on February 28, 2026, at the very start of a short but intense US-Israel war on Iran. His son, Mojtaba Khamenei, was named successor by Iran's Assembly of Experts in March but has stayed out of public view since. A fragile calm has since returned to the region, including the vital Strait of Hormuz shipping lane.
stories.what_happened: Four months after his death, Iran began a seven-day state funeral (July 4–9) stretching across five cities in Iran and beyond, with millions of mourners expected; the body will be buried July 9 at the Imam Reza shrine in Mashhad. Separately, reports emerged that the US privately warned Iran that Israel might try to assassinate Iranian negotiators — including the foreign minister and parliament speaker — during peace talks, a sign of friction between Washington and its ally over how far to push Tehran.
stories.why_it_matters: The funeral is the first big state event under the untested new leader, Mojtaba Khamenei, and Iran is using its scale to project strength and unity after a humiliating war. How power actually transfers in a secretive theocracy affects the stability of the entire oil-rich Gulf.
stories.ripple_effects: A stable, negotiating Iran keeps oil flowing and the Strait of Hormuz — the chokepoint for roughly a fifth of the world's oil — open, which helps oil importers like India. Any renewed assassination or breakdown in talks could reignite conflict and spike crude prices worldwide.
stories.why_now: The funeral was repeatedly postponed because of the war and its aftermath; scheduling it now, with US-Iran talks paused for the mourning period, lets the new leadership consolidate control and stage a display of legitimacy.
stories.watch_next: Whether US-Iran nuclear and peace talks resume after July 9, and any sign of how much real authority Mojtaba Khamenei actually holds.

key_terms.definition: Iran's most powerful position — a cleric who outranks the president and controls the military, judiciary and foreign policy.
A narrow sea passage between Iran and Oman through which about 20% of the world's oil is shipped; any threat to it moves global oil prices.

stories.the_lesson: Markets care less about who leads a country than about whether the transition is orderly — predictability keeps oil and risk premiums low, while uncertainty at the top of an oil power is priced into the whole world's fuel bill.
stories.market_link: A calm Iran and open Hormuz have helped pull crude back toward $68 — any re-escalation would reverse that and hit oil-importing economies and airlines.

stories.title: A weak US jobs report flips the world's biggest rate question
stories.background: The US Federal Reserve sets interest rates for the world's largest economy, and its decisions ripple into every market. Unusually, for months the fear has been that the Fed might have to RAISE rates — because an oil-price shock from the Iran war pushed inflation up. Investors have been watching every data point for whether hikes are coming.
stories.what_happened: The US added just 57,000 jobs in June — about half of the ~115,000 economists expected — and prior months were revised down by 74,000. The unemployment rate actually fell to 4.2%, but for the 'wrong' reason: people gave up looking for work, dropping the labour-force participation rate to its lowest in 50 years outside the Covid era. Markets read a weakening job market as a reason the Fed won't hike: the odds of a July hike collapsed to around 22%, gold jumped back above $4,100 an ounce, and the blue-chip Dow Jones index hit a record high.
stories.why_it_matters: A cooling labour market takes the threat of rate hikes off the table, and cheaper money tends to lift stocks, gold and other assets. But it also signals the US economy may be losing momentum — fewer jobs means weaker demand ahead, which is a double-edged relief.
stories.ripple_effects: Lower US rate expectations tend to weaken the dollar and can send money flowing back toward emerging markets like India. Gold rallies because it pays no interest, so it looks better when rates aren't rising. Rate-sensitive value stocks (in the Dow) outperformed high-priced tech (in the Nasdaq), a 'rotation' of leadership.
stories.why_now: June's report landed just before the US July 4 holiday and ahead of the Fed's July 29 meeting, so it directly reshaped bets on what the Fed does next.
stories.watch_next: The next US inflation reading and the July 29 Fed meeting — whether 'no hike' hardens into talk of eventual cuts.

key_terms.definition: The share of working-age people either working or actively looking for work; when it falls, a lower jobless rate can be misleading because people have simply stopped searching.
The monthly count of jobs added across the US economy (excluding farms) — the single most-watched gauge of labour-market health.

stories.the_lesson: Markets price the future, not the present: 'bad' economic news can lift stocks if it changes what investors expect the central bank to do — a weak jobs report can rally shares simply by removing the fear of higher rates.
stories.market_link: Fading hike odds lifted gold (+2%), sent the Dow to a record, weakened the dollar, and could ease pressure on the rupee and support foreign inflows to India.

stories.title: India's driest June in over a century leaves farmers on edge
stories.background: India depends on the June–September monsoon for about 70% of its annual rainfall, and roughly half of its farmland has no irrigation — so the rains decide the fate of the summer 'kharif' crop (rice, pulses, cotton, oilseeds) and much of rural income. A weak monsoon can dent food output, push up prices, and squeeze the hundreds of millions who live off farming.
stories.what_happened: India just recorded one of its driest Junes in more than a century — by some measures the third-lowest rainfall in 126 years — with a rainfall deficit of around 46% in the first three weeks. Drought conditions are spreading across the country, delaying kharif sowing in states like Maharashtra, where authorities told farmers to wait before planting. The government says grain stocks are ample to handle any shortfall.
stories.why_it_matters: Weak, delayed rains threaten crop yields and rural demand — a big chunk of India's consumer economy. If food output falls, food inflation can rise, which hits household budgets and complicates the Reserve Bank of India's interest-rate decisions.
stories.ripple_effects: Lower farm output can lift prices of staples, hurt sales of everything from tractors to soap in rural areas, and weigh on companies that sell to villages. It can also increase imports of edible oils and pulses, widening the trade gap and adding pressure on the rupee.
stories.why_now: The monsoon advanced late and unevenly this year; forecasters now expect the season near 90% of its long-term average, which would make 2026 India's weakest monsoon in more than a decade.
stories.watch_next: July–August rainfall — the make-or-break months; a strong revival could still rescue the crop, a continued deficit would deepen the food-inflation risk.

key_terms.definition: India's monsoon-season crops (sown June–July, harvested autumn) — rice, pulses, cotton, soybeans — whose success hinges on timely rain.
The rate at which food prices rise; because food is a large part of Indian household spending, it heavily influences overall inflation and RBI policy.

stories.the_lesson: In farm-dependent economies, weather is a macro variable: a bad monsoon can do as much to inflation and growth as any central-bank decision, which is why investors watch the rain gauge as closely as the rate-setter.
stories.market_link: A weak monsoon pressures agri-input, tractor and rural-focused FMCG stocks, can lift food prices, and adds to the case for the RBI to stay cautious.

stories.title: Foreign investors flee Indian stocks; rupee sits near a record low
stories.background: Foreign Portfolio Investors (FPIs) — big overseas funds — buy and sell Indian shares in huge sizes, so their flows swing the market and the currency. When they sell Indian stocks, they convert rupees back to dollars, which weakens the rupee. India also imports most of its oil, so a weak rupee makes fuel dearer.
stories.what_happened: FPIs pulled about ₹49,340 crore (roughly $5.8 billion) out of Indian equities in June, taking 2026's total outflows to about ₹2.7 lakh crore — already more than the whole of 2025. The rupee has been grinding weaker, closing around 95.2–95.3 per US dollar, near record-low territory, pressured by importer dollar demand and the foreign selling. Yet domestic buying kept the Sensex and Nifty rising (Sensex around 77,800, Nifty around 24,300).
stories.why_it_matters: It's a tug-of-war: foreigners are cashing out of India while local investors keep buying, and for now the locals are winning on the index level. But a persistently weak rupee raises the cost of imports (especially oil), can stoke inflation, and erodes the dollar value of any gains for foreign holders.
stories.ripple_effects: A weaker rupee helps exporters (like IT firms paid in dollars) but hurts importers and anyone with dollar debt. Heavy FPI selling can cap how far Indian indices rise and keeps the currency and bond markets on edge.
stories.why_now: Global funds have been rotating money toward other markets and the stronger dollar era; a softer US rate outlook after the weak jobs report could, if it holds, slow or reverse those outflows.
stories.watch_next: Whether the fading US rate-hike fears bring FPIs back to India, and whether the RBI steps in to defend the rupee near 95.5.

key_terms.definition: Money that foreign funds move in and out of a country's stocks and bonds; large outflows pressure both the market and the local currency.
When the rupee buys fewer dollars; it makes imports (like oil) costlier and can add to inflation, but helps exporters earning in dollars.

stories.the_lesson: A rising stock index and a falling currency can happen at once — they answer different questions. The index reflects local optimism; the currency reflects the balance of dollars flowing in and out. Watch both to see the full picture.
stories.market_link: Continued FPI selling and a soft rupee pressure rate-sensitive and import-heavy Indian stocks, while dollar-earning IT exporters get a cushion.

stories.title: India yanks apps that let strangers remotely switch off e-rickshaws
stories.background: India's streets run on millions of cheap electric rickshaws — three-wheeled taxis that are a lifeline income for poor drivers. Many use battery-management systems (BMS) that connect over Bluetooth to a phone app to monitor charge. If those connections aren't secured, they can be hijacked.
stories.what_happened: After videos went viral of drivers stranded as their vehicles were remotely disabled, the government ordered app stores to remove at least three apps — including a Chinese 'BAT-BMS' app — that miscreants were exploiting to cut power to e-rickshaws by connecting to unsecured Bluetooth battery systems. Authorities urged app stores to tighten their vetting.
stories.why_it_matters: It's a small story with a big lesson about the risks of cheap, insecure 'smart' hardware: when a poor driver's livelihood can be switched off by a stranger with a phone, everyday digital infrastructure becomes a safety and even national-security issue. The China link also feeds India's broader wariness of Chinese-made connected tech.
stories.ripple_effects: Expect tighter rules on Bluetooth-enabled devices and battery systems, more scrutiny of Chinese-linked apps, and pressure on manufacturers to secure their hardware. It fits a pattern of India policing connected Chinese technology on security grounds.
stories.why_now: Viral videos of a helpless driver in tears forced a fast government response and put a spotlight on a vulnerability that had been quietly exploited.
stories.watch_next: Whether the government mandates security standards for BMS/IoT devices, and any wider action against Chinese-linked apps.

key_terms.definition: Electronics that monitor and control a battery's charging and safety; if it's remotely accessible and unsecured, it can be tampered with.
Everyday devices connected to the internet or phones; convenient, but each connection is a potential door for hackers if not secured.

stories.the_lesson: Every connected device is a potential attack surface — convenience features like remote control become vulnerabilities the moment they aren't properly secured, and the poorest users often bear the cost.

stories.title: India signs a trade deal with New Zealand and hardens its terror line with Japan
stories.background: India has been racing to sign free-trade agreements (FTAs) — pacts that cut tariffs and open markets — to diversify its trade and reduce reliance on any single partner, especially amid US tariff pressure. It also uses diplomacy to isolate Pakistan over cross-border militancy, a long-running dispute centred on Kashmir.
stories.what_happened: India and New Zealand signed a 'historic' free-trade agreement — one of the fastest India has ever negotiated, concluded in about ten months — to be implemented later this year. Separately, in a joint statement, India and Japan condemned 'Pakistan-backed cross-border terrorism' and cited a UN report implicating a militant group in the April 2025 Pahalgam attack on tourists in Kashmir.
stories.why_it_matters: The FTA gives Indian exporters new access to a developed market and signals India's push to lock in trade deals while global trade turns protectionist. The Japan statement shows India building a coalition of major economies behind its security narrative on Pakistan.
stories.ripple_effects: FTAs can boost specific export sectors (like textiles, agriculture and manufactured goods) and deepen supply-chain ties. Joint condemnations raise diplomatic pressure on Pakistan and strengthen the India-Japan strategic partnership, part of the wider effort to counterbalance China in the Indo-Pacific.
stories.why_now: With the US keeping trade partners on 'short leashes' (Trump just declined to fully renew the North America pact) and a July 24 deadline looming on India-US talks, New Delhi is diversifying its trade relationships fast.
stories.watch_next: The India-US interim trade deal (July 24 deadline) and the details/implementation timeline of the India–New Zealand FTA.

key_terms.definition: A deal between countries to reduce or remove tariffs and barriers, making it cheaper to trade goods and services with each other.
Attacks planned or launched from one country against another; India accuses Pakistan of backing militants operating in Kashmir, which Pakistan denies.

stories.the_lesson: When the biggest trading power turns protectionist, mid-sized economies hedge by signing more deals with each other — trade diversification is a country's insurance policy against a single partner's mood swings.
stories.market_link: New FTAs can benefit Indian export-oriented sectors (textiles, agri, manufacturing); progress on trade deals tends to support market sentiment and the rupee.

stories.title: Google loses its record EU antitrust appeal — a €4.1bn bill
stories.background: The European Union has been the world's most aggressive regulator of Big Tech. Back in 2018 it fined Google a record amount for abusing the dominance of Android — the operating system on most of the world's smartphones — by requiring phone makers to pre-install Google's search and Chrome browser to get access to the app store. Google has been appealing ever since.
stories.what_happened: The EU's top courts largely upheld the case, and Google has now lost its long-running appeal, leaving it on the hook for about €4.1 billion (roughly $4.7 billion). Google said the ruling 'fails to recognise' its investment in keeping Android open.
stories.why_it_matters: It's a landmark confirmation that bundling your own services to entrench dominance can be illegal — a precedent that shapes how gatekeeper platforms must behave in Europe and beyond. The sum is huge, but the bigger cost is the constraint on how Google can package its products.
stories.ripple_effects: It strengthens the EU's hand as it enforces its newer Digital Markets Act against Apple, Meta, Amazon and others, and emboldens regulators elsewhere. For consumers, it pushes toward more choice of default search engines and browsers on phones.
stories.why_now: This is the culmination of an eight-year legal fight — the final appeal ruling — landing as the EU simultaneously ramps up its broader Big Tech crackdown.
stories.watch_next: Whether Google seeks any further legal avenue, and how the ruling shapes ongoing EU cases against other tech giants.

key_terms.definition: Laws that stop companies from abusing a dominant market position to crush competition or lock in customers unfairly.
Forcing products to be sold or installed together (e.g. Android + Google Search) to leverage strength in one area into another.

stories.the_lesson: Market dominance itself isn't illegal — using it to smother rivals is. Platforms that grow by making their own services the unavoidable default eventually collide with competition law.
stories.market_link: Big antitrust rulings pressure Alphabet and set precedents that weigh on the regulatory-risk premium for all mega-cap platform stocks (Apple, Meta, Amazon).

stories.title: Big Tech's new AI land-grab: sending armies of engineers into clients' offices
stories.background: The AI boom's next phase is about 'deployment' — getting companies to actually use AI, not just talk about it. The big players have realised that selling AI models isn't enough; customers need heavy hand-holding to make them work. So the giants are spending billions to embed their own engineers directly inside customer offices.
stories.what_happened: Microsoft launched a $2.5 billion 'Frontier Company' to help firms adopt AI — the largest such move yet — joining OpenAI's roughly $4 billion 'Deployment Company', Anthropic's ~$1.5 billion venture and Amazon's ~$1 billion effort, for nearly $9 billion combined. Separately, OpenAI reportedly offered the US government a 5% stake as it courts political goodwill.
stories.why_it_matters: It's a sign the AI race is shifting from building models to proving they deliver real business value — and that value, the giants are betting, only shows up with intensive, in-person deployment. Whoever gets embedded deepest in customers' workflows wins the long-term relationship.
stories.ripple_effects: It could squeeze independent consulting firms and 'AI cloud' middlemen — shares of firms like Nebius and CoreWeave fell after Meta detailed its own AI-cloud plans. It also concentrates AI power further among a handful of giants, raising the stakes of the antitrust and ownership debates.
stories.why_now: After two years of massive AI spending, investors are demanding proof of returns — pushing the giants to guarantee results by putting boots on the ground in client offices.
stories.watch_next: Whether these deployment bets translate into faster revenue growth — and the reaction of the 'AI cloud' providers being disintermediated.

key_terms.definition: The work of actually integrating AI tools into a company's day-to-day operations — harder than it sounds, hence the need for embedded engineers.
Cutting out the middlemen — here, big AI firms going straight to customers, threatening the consultants and cloud resellers in between.

stories.the_lesson: In a technology gold rush, the money eventually flows to whoever controls the last mile to the customer — owning the deployment relationship can matter more than owning the underlying tech.
stories.market_link: The deployment push benefits Microsoft, Amazon and OpenAI's backers but pressures 'AI cloud' names (Nebius, CoreWeave) and independent IT-services firms, including India's.

stories.title: Killer heat grips the US and Europe as summers turn extreme
stories.background: Human-caused climate change is making heatwaves more frequent, more intense and longer. Heat is now the deadliest weather hazard in many rich countries, quietly killing far more people than storms or floods — often the elderly, the sick, and outdoor workers.
stories.what_happened: A 'dangerous' heatwave is baking the eastern United States over the July 4 holiday weekend, with some areas nearing record highs, while Europe is enduring an extreme summer: France reported a roughly 30% rise in deaths during its June heatwave, and England just logged its warmest June since records began in 1865. The WHO warned Europe must 'plan for heat like winter flu'.
stories.why_it_matters: Extreme heat kills, strains power grids and transport, cuts worker productivity, and drives surging demand for air conditioning — which itself burns more energy. It is becoming a recurring economic and public-health shock, not a one-off.
stories.ripple_effects: Heatwaves spike electricity demand (and prices), stress crops and water supplies, and boost sales of cooling gear — Europe's air-conditioner demand is so high it's straining trade ties with China, the main supplier. Grids and railways built for cooler climates buckle under the stress.
stories.why_now: Successive record-hot months and back-to-back deadly heatwaves are pushing governments and health bodies to treat heat as a predictable seasonal emergency rather than a surprise.
stories.watch_next: Peak temperatures and any heat-related death tolls over the US holiday weekend, and Europe's next forecast heatwave.

key_terms.definition: A prolonged period of excessively hot weather; defined relative to what's normal for a place, and increasingly linked to climate change.
The number of deaths above what would normally be expected in a period — used to measure a heatwave's true toll, since heat often kills indirectly.

stories.the_lesson: Heat is a slow-motion disaster: it rarely makes dramatic footage, but it kills more people and costs economies more than flashier weather events — which is why it's now treated as critical infrastructure risk.
stories.market_link: Heat spikes power and cooling demand (utilities, AC makers), pressures grids and crops, and feeds the Europe-China trade friction over air conditioners.

also_notable.title: Ebola outbreak rages in DR Congo as Uganda confirms a Marburg case
also_notable.why: Two dangerous haemorrhagic-fever viruses spreading in central Africa — a reminder that localized outbreaks can become regional health emergencies.

also_notable.title: Tesla beats Q2 delivery estimates as Europe demand recovers
also_notable.why: A record second-quarter for deliveries suggests Tesla's core car business is regaining momentum after two years of decline — though the stock still fell sharply.

also_notable.title: Trump declines to fully renew the USMCA North America trade pact
also_notable.why: Keeping the deal on a short 'annual review' leash injects uncertainty into North American trade — part of a wider protectionist turn.

also_notable.title: Venezuela earthquake survivor pulled out alive after eight days
also_notable.why: A rare survival story from last week's devastating quakes, as anger grows over the government's response and building standards.

disclaimer: Decoded from public reporting for general understanding. Verify before relying on any single claim; details evolve.
